quoteFirst: Lewin himself, after studying the _Admiralty Tide Tables_ for the year 1859, admits that ‘on January 14th, being the fourth day before the full moon, high water at Dover is at
quoteLast: ’, and that it may possibly have occurred as early on the day of Caesar’s landing.
title: Ancient Britain and the Invasions of Julius Caesar
author: Holmes, T. Rice (Thomas Rice), 1855-1933

quoteFirst: As we have already seen, he admits[2993] that on a day when high tide occurred at 7.31 a.m. the stream might turn eastward at 4.26 or even at 4 p.m. If he is right, it follows that on a day when high tide occurred at
quoteLast: the stream might turn eastward at 2.26 or even at 2 p.m. To make this admission, from which, if he had been confronted with his own words, he could by no subtlety have escaped, would have been to throw up his case; and such candour would have been too much to expect from a professional advocate.
title: Ancient Britain and the Invasions of Julius Caesar
author: Holmes, T. Rice (Thomas Rice), 1855-1933

quoteFirst: [2994] According to these data, if, on the day of Caesar’s landing, high tide had occurred at
quoteLast: , the westward stream might have ceased at 3.4 p.m. It would have been impossible for Lewin, if he had been required to take account of this statement, to deny it.
title: Ancient Britain and the Invasions of Julius Caesar
author: Holmes, T. Rice (Thomas Rice), 1855-1933

title: The Chautauquan, Vol. 05, November 1884, No. 2
